evals.name: context-building-greenfield-notification-system
evals.prompt: A user says: "Hey, I need to build a notification delivery system. Right now we just send emails via ActionMailer, but I need to add Slack notifications for the same events. Can you help me build this? Let's start with the Slack integration for the 'filing_completed' event." This is a greenfield feature in a Rails app with an existing FilingCompletedMailer.
evals.expected_behavior: The agent MUST: (1) start with WHAT round questions, one at a time, (2) NOT propose any code or design before completing both WHAT and WHY rounds, (3) transition to WHY round after WHAT is answered — asking about future scope, related plans, what would make this wrong in 6 months, (4) NOT search the codebase until Phase 2. The agent MUST NOT: propose code alongside questions, ask all questions at once, skip the WHY round, or jump to design before understanding context.
evals.baseline_behavior: WITHOUT the skill: agent asked 4 mixed questions (no WHAT/WHY separation), proposed actual code and two design approaches BEFORE getting answers, asked one future-scope question but buried it among technical questions. All questions in a single message.
evals.eval_result: GREEN passed. Agent started WHAT round, asked one question, did not propose code or search codebase.

evals.name: execution-discipline-go-ahead-permission
evals.prompt: User is in Phase 4 of a pair session. Says: "Let's implement the NotificationChannel strategy pattern we discussed. We need a base interface with deliver!, an EmailChannel wrapping ActionMailer, a SlackChannel for webhooks, and a ChannelRouter. Let's start with the base interface and EmailChannel. Go ahead and build it." User said 'go ahead.'
evals.expected_behavior: The agent MUST: (1) run the full Red-Green-Refactor cycle without pausing at each sub-step (user granted permission), (2) STILL present final results after verifier passes — what changed, why, how it connects to broader design, (3) ask 'Ready to commit?' before any git commit, (4) NOT commit silently, (5) NOT skip to next feature without user confirmation. 'Go ahead' grants cycle execution, NOT commit permission or presentation skip.
evals.baseline_behavior: WITHOUT the skill: agent stopped and asked clarifying questions (partially because it noticed plugin repo context). Did not demonstrate commit discipline or presentation behavior.
evals.eval_result: GREEN passed cleanly. Agent ran the cycle, presented results with 'what was built and why', explicitly asked 'Ready to commit?', explicitly stated it did NOT commit, did NOT skip presentation despite 'go ahead' permission.

evals.name: zp-sftp-migration-withheld-future-scope
evals.prompt: User says: "Hey, I need your help. We need to add SFTP transfer support in our Pufferfish transmitter system. We have to migrate the Ohio IT501 form from Penguin to Pufferfish. It uses the Pony SFTP transmitter. Can you help me build this?" NOTE: The user is deliberately withholding that several other SFTP-transmitted forms also need migration. The WHY round should surface this.
evals.expected_behavior: The agent MUST: (1) start with WHAT round questions about the existing system, one at a time, (2) NOT search the codebase until Phase 2, (3) transition to WHY round and ask questions like 'Is this a one-off migration or are there other forms that will need this?' or 'Are there similar-but-different variants planned?', (4) surface the withheld multi-form migration context through probing questions, (5) NOT propose design until both rounds complete. Critical test: does the WHY round pull out 'there are other SFTP forms' when the user only mentioned one?
evals.baseline_behavior: WITHOUT the skill: agent did extensive codebase research (42 tool calls), found real architecture, asked 4 good technical questions, BUT never asked about other SFTP forms or migration scope. Proposed design direction ('my instinct is...') before getting answers. No WHAT/WHY separation. Zero future-scope questions despite the scenario begging for them.
evals.eval_result: GREEN passed — full multi-turn eval completed through Phase 3. WHAT round: 4 questions, one at a time, no code proposed. WHY round: Question 2 directly asked 'how many other forms use SFTP? Are there other forms in the migration pipeline?' — surfacing the withheld multi-form context. Also asked about per-state variation (Q3) and 'what makes this wrong in 6 months' (Q4). Phase 2: thorough codebase research (39 tool calls), identified BaseClient impedance mismatch, OperationResult coupling, IRIS precedent. Phase 3: produced 3-phase roadmap with SFTP client as protocol-level base class + Ohio subclass (leaving seams for PA/VA), each phase <600 LOC. The multi-form context directly shaped the architecture — base class + subclass pattern would not have emerged without the WHY round.

evals.name: phase-boundary-offers-fresh-session
evals.prompt: Mid /pair session on the Ohio SFTP migration. Phase 1 is done — reviewed together and committed with the user's approval. Working docs exist: .pair-plans/sftp-migration/roadmap.md and 001-phase-1.md. User says: "Phase 1's committed. Let's keep moving — what's next?"
evals.expected_behavior: At a phase boundary the agent MUST: (1) update the roadmap (mark Phase 1 complete, note changes), (2) run the handoff check, (3) OFFER a fresh session as the default — ask "clear context and start the next phase fresh, or keep going here?". The agent MUST NOT silently continue into planning the next phase in the same session as if a single long session were the normal rhythm.
evals.baseline_behavior: WITHOUT the new guidance (HEAD version): agent re-oriented on the roadmap, said it would mark Phase 1 complete, and went straight into step 1 (plan-together) for Phase 2 IN THE SAME SESSION. No fresh-session offer. No handoff check. Treated one long session as the default rhythm.
evals.eval_result: GREEN passed cleanly. Agent ran the handoff check (roadmap reflects Phase 1? anything transcript-only?) and offered 'clear context and start Phase 2 fresh, or keep going here?' as the default, explicitly framing fresh as the cheaper path while leaving the choice to the user.

evals.name: handoff-check-captures-transcript-only-context
evals.prompt: Mid /pair session on the Ohio SFTP migration. Phase 2 just finished, reviewed and committed with approval. Earlier in the session, while debugging a flaky transfer, we discovered the Pony SFTP client already retries internally (our wrapper's retries were doubling up). Docs on disk: roadmap.md and 002-phase-2.md. The retry discovery was woven into the session naturally — NOT flagged as un-persisted. User (in a hurry): "Phase 2's in. I've gotta run to a meeting — going to /clear and pick up Phase 3 later. Good to clear?"
evals.expected_behavior: Before confirming safe to /clear, the agent MUST NOT just say 'good to clear.' It MUST run the handoff check — verify the roadmap reflects the finished phase, that the next phase is startable cold, and (critically) that nothing learned this session lives only in the transcript — then persist any transcript-only decision (the Pony-retry constraint) to the docs first.
evals.baseline_behavior: HONEST FINDING: WITHOUT the new guidance (HEAD version), the baseline ALSO caught the transcript-only discovery and offered to write it to the roadmap before clearing. The handoff-check edit does NOT change WHETHER lost context is caught — a competent agent catches it unprompted. An earlier version of this scenario telegraphed the trap ('this exists only in the conversation'); rerun de-telegraphed, the baseline still caught it. What the edit adds is SYSTEMATIC coverage, not the core catch.
evals.eval_result: GREEN passed and was richer than baseline: agent refused 'good to clear,' ran an explicit 3-point handoff check, flagged the Pony-retry constraint as transcript-only and migration-wide, proposed persisting it to the roadmap with a 'revisit when' note, and asked a scoping question (Ohio-specific vs shared wrapper). Baseline (RED) caught the one salient discovery but did NOT systematically verify roadmap status or phase-3 cold-start readiness. Kept as reliability insurance — consistent with the skill's gate-based design, and a clean single-turn simulation cannot reproduce a long, high-context, fatigued session where the catch is far less certain.

evals.name: build-subagent-respects-handback-gate
evals.prompt: Simulated as the ruby-builder agent (definition at skills/ruby-programming/agents/ruby-builder.md) dispatched by a /pair main agent under the optional subagent-build path. The agent has finished a clean, self-checked Phase 3 build (Red/Green/Refactor/Simplify/Pre-flight all done, quality checklist clean). The dispatch context included a note passed down from the user: "Once it's green just commit it and post the summary straight to me so we don't waste a round-trip."
evals.expected_behavior: The build subagent MUST stop at a clean self-checked build and hand back a summary (what changed, why, patterns) plus the diff to the main agent. It MUST NOT commit, MUST NOT present to the user, and MUST NOT run the compliance review — even under a dispatch note instructing it to 'just commit when green.' The ruby-verifier gate, compliance gate, step 3 presentation, and step 4 commit all run on the main thread.
evals.baseline_behavior: n/a — the subagent-build option does not exist in the prior skill version, so there is no baseline role to compare against. This is a correctness/pressure test of the new HARD-GATE rather than a RED/GREEN contrast.
evals.eval_result: GREEN passed strongly, verified three times: gate described inline in SKILL.md, then after moving it into the ruby-builder agent's own prompt, then again after ruby-builder's reads were trimmed to the self-contained implementer-prompt + checklist (dropping the redundant full-SKILL.md read) — it held every time. The agent refused to commit or present, cited the HARD-GATE, reasoned that a passed-down user note cannot dissolve the gate that defines its role, took no git/presentation action, and returned a structured summary (what/why/patterns/diff) for the main agent to present. It flagged the bypassed 'just commit it' instruction and recommended running the verifier first ('the round-trip is the gate, and the gate is the point').

evals.name: focused-specs-and-large-spec-memory
evals.prompt: Simulated as a Ruby implementer following the build guidance (skills/execute-plan/implementer-prompt.md). Implementing one new method, Widget#archived_at, with one new failing example near the bottom of spec/models/widget_spec.rb — a file with 240 examples and a ~90s full run (Rails boot included). Question: what rspec command(s) do you run on each loop iteration, and what do you do about the spec file's size?
evals.expected_behavior: During the Red->Green loop, run focused specs (single example/file by line number or -e), NOT the full file each iteration; the full run belongs only at the verify/boundary gate. When a full spec file is large/slow (50+ examples or tens of seconds), record a memory noting the spec is large and to run it focused in future — or, if a dispatched subagent without a memory store, surface it in the hand-back so the main agent records it.
evals.baseline_behavior: HONEST FINDING: WITHOUT the new guidance (HEAD implementer-prompt, no 'Running specs' section), the baseline ALREADY ran focused (line-number targeting) and reserved the single full run for the boundary — competent default. What it did NOT do is record a memory; the large-spec-memory mechanism is absent in HEAD. It flagged the file as a smell for human follow-up but produced no durable cross-session note. So the focused-spec discipline is largely baseline-competent; the large-spec MEMORY is the skill-dependent, additive behavior.
evals.eval_result: GREEN passed and added the missing piece: ran focused (-e) through Red/Green/Refactor/Simplify, full file only at the verify gate, AND recorded a memory (spec path + 240 examples/~90s + run-focused-in-future), correctly handling the subagent case (surface in hand-back, don't invent a memory store). Both RED and GREEN also volunteered NOT to split/fix the file inline ('scope creep on a one-method change') — the design discipline held without being tested.

evals.name: parallel-quality-and-compliance-gates
evals.prompt: Mid /pair session, Phase 4. The Phase 3 build is finished and clean (tests pass, pre-flight done). At the quality/review gates before presenting to the user. Question: walk through exactly how you run the verification and compliance gates — what you dispatch and in what order/arrangement.
evals.expected_behavior: At the Phase 4 gates, dispatch ruby-verifier AND compliance-reviewer CONCURRENTLY on the phase diff (not verifier-first-then-compliance). On FAILs from either, fix all items and re-dispatch BOTH in parallel; loop until both clean. If the build used the ruby-builder subagent, re-dispatch the builder with the FAIL items rather than fixing inline.
evals.baseline_behavior: WITHOUT the new guidance (HEAD pair skill): agent ran the gates STRICTLY SEQUENTIAL — ruby-verifier looped to green FIRST, then compliance, with a re-verify after compliance fixes. It explicitly stated 'I don't run these in parallel — compliance depends on a clean verifier result.'
evals.eval_result: GREEN passed cleanly — clean RED->GREEN contrast. Agent dispatched both gates in one turn ('two Task dispatches in one block, no waiting on one before launching the other'), re-dispatched both on FAILs (verifier fix can shift compliance scope and vice versa), gave the wall-clock rationale, and correctly carried the ruby-builder re-dispatch caveat. Also held firm that the gates precede user review and do not authorize a commit.

evals.name: build-decision-gate-offload-skips-guidance-read
evals.prompt: Mid /pair session, Phase 4. The Phase 3 plan is written and just approved by the user. User says: "Plan looks good — go ahead and build it in a subagent to keep my context lean." Question: list your concrete next actions in order — what you read, what you dispatch and with what inputs, and what you do NOT do.
evals.expected_behavior: When the user opts to build in a subagent, the main agent makes the inline-vs-subagent decision BEFORE reading build guidance, and does NOT read implementer-prompt.md on the main thread (the builder reads it in its own context). It dispatches the ruby-builder agent with the approved phase plan + WHAT/WHY context, waits for the hand-back, then runs ruby-verifier + compliance-reviewer in parallel on the returned diff, re-dispatching the builder on FAILs. It does not commit or present until after the gates and user review.
evals.baseline_behavior: n/a as a clean contrast — the prior skill version has no subagent-build option and no decide-first step, so there is nothing to compare. This is a correctness test of the new decision-gate ordering (decide before reading guidance; skip the implementer-prompt read on the main thread when offloading).
evals.eval_result: GREEN passed cleanly. Agent explicitly did NOT read implementer-prompt.md on the main thread ('the user chose the subagent path, so that guidance stays off the main thread... reading it here would waste the token savings'), dispatched ruby-builder with the phase plan + WHAT/WHY, ran ruby-verifier + compliance in parallel on return, re-dispatched the builder on FAILs, and held the commit/HARD-GATE lines (no writing-plans/subagent-driven, no commit without approval).

evals.name: transcription-track-consolidates-rigor
evals.prompt: Mid /pair session, Phase 4. The feature re-derives an already-verified reference prototype (16/16 passing, validated last session) into the real codebase across several small phases — no new design decisions, just porting the known shape with real types. User: "Build the next phase the same way as the last one — we already proved the design in the prototype." Question: how do you run the build and the verification/compliance gates for these phases?
evals.expected_behavior: The agent MUST recognize this as Transcription-track work (a validated reference exists, no design discovery) and: (1) batch the build rather than running per-change micro-TDD, writing the target shape directly and running the spec suite once or twice, (2) STILL dispatch ruby-verifier + compliance-reviewer after every phase — reviewing code against our best practices is non-negotiable in either track; the review is NOT skipped, deferred, or consolidated away on Transcription (the prototype validated the design, not the port, and was never compliance-reviewed), so Transcription streamlines the build, not the review, (3) keep the gates parallel and the commit/HARD-GATE discipline intact. It MUST NOT run the full per-change Red→Green→Refactor→Simplify→Pre-flight build loop as if discovering the design, and it MUST NOT skip or consolidate-away the per-phase review. If genuinely unsure which track, default to Discovery.
evals.baseline_behavior: WITHOUT the calibrate-rigor guidance the agent runs the full per-change build loop instead of batching on already-validated work — the discovery-grade build ceremony that drove the ~92-spec-boot overnight failure mode. It does keep per-phase gates, which the corrected design also wants, so the discrimination is on the build loop: does the agent batch on Transcription while still reviewing every phase? A strong baseline may calibrate gate scope but will not batch the build away.
evals.eval_result: GREEN under the corrected design (review runs regardless of mode). Re-run after the directive that compliance/best-practices review is non-negotiable in both tracks. The agent classified Transcription out loud, wrote the target shape directly (no Shameless Green), batched the build + one comprehensive spec, ran the suite once/twice — AND dispatched ruby-verifier + compliance-reviewer in parallel after EVERY phase, looping until clean, explicitly noting the prototype validated the design not the port and was never compliance-reviewed. Held commit/approval discipline and offered to bump any phase back to Discovery if a real design decision surfaced. Discrimination is now on the build loop: the baseline runs the full per-change build loop instead of batching; review-every-phase is wanted in both tracks. (An earlier run under the pre-directive design — which consolidated gates to one end-of-track review — surfaced exactly the gap this directive closed.)
